coredata.dc:title: Study on the therapeutic activity of [BMIM]-PF6-IL assisted Er2O3 NPs synthesized by polyol method against pathogenic bacterium as well as MCF-7 breast tumor cells
coredata.dc:creator: Azhagar, Surya
kumar Chinnalagu, Dhilip
Mayakrishnan, Arumugam
Sundrarajan, Mahalingam
coredata.dc:description: This work emphasizes ionic liquid (IL) initiated erbium oxide Er2O3 (IL-EO) nanoparticles (NPs) synthesized utilizing a simple one-step modified polyol approach. The synthesized IL-EO NPs were elucidated using FT-IR, XRD, UV–Vis DRS, FE-SEM accompanied with EDX and HR-TEM spectroscopy. The capping agent BMIM-PF6 IL was used to effectively slowdown the growth rate. The results showed that adding BMIM-PF6 IL successfully generated more oxygen vacancies, which aids in the production of singlet oxygen, superoxide and hydroxyl radicals (1O2, *O2 – & *OH). The gram-positive bacterium, S. aureus and gram-negative bacterium, E. coli were eradicated with a low concentration of 25 μg/mL of IL-EO NPs to confirm antibacterial activity. The potential biocidal efficiency of IL-EO NPs against S. aureus (Zone of inhibition-12 mm dia.) and E. coli (Zone of inhibition-14 mm dia.). Likewise, the IL-EO NPs exemplified better cell lysis towards MCF-7 breast cancer with an IC50 of 44.83 μg/mL. The results indicated IL-EO NPs inhibits the growth of tested bacterial and cancer cells with a series of mechanism. The binding of cells occurs through van-der-Waals interaction that ruptures the negative surface charge on the cell wall. The main mechanism includes the release of reactive oxygen species (ROS) causing severe damage to mitochondrial Deoxyribonucleic Acid (mtDNA) and sugar backbone of Deoxyribonucleic Acid (DNA), lipids and proteins. We strongly recommend that IL-EO NPs can effectively act as an anticancer drug, especially in treating the MCF-7 human breast tumor cell.

head.ce:title: Study on the therapeutic activity of [BMIM]-PF6-IL assisted Er2O3 NPs synthesized by polyol method against pathogenic bacterium as well as MCF-7 breast tumor cells

ce:abstract-sec.ce:simple-para: This work emphasizes ionic liquid (IL) initiated erbium oxide Er2O3 (IL-EO) nanoparticles (NPs) synthesized utilizing a simple one-step modified polyol approach. The synthesized IL-EO NPs were elucidated using FT-IR, XRD, UV–Vis DRS, FE-SEM accompanied with EDX and HR-TEM spectroscopy. The capping agent BMIM-PF6 IL was used to effectively slowdown the growth rate. The results showed that adding BMIM-PF6 IL successfully generated more oxygen vacancies, which aids in the production of singlet oxygen, superoxide and hydroxyl radicals (1O2, *O2 – & *OH). The gram-positive bacterium, S. aureus and gram-negative bacterium, E. coli were eradicated with a low concentration of 25 μg/mL of IL-EO NPs to confirm antibacterial activity. The potential biocidal efficiency of IL-EO NPs against S. aureus (Zone of inhibition-12 mm dia.) and E. coli (Zone of inhibition-14 mm dia.). Likewise, the IL-EO NPs exemplified better cell lysis towards MCF-7 breast cancer with an IC50 of 44.83 μg/mL. The results indicated IL-EO NPs inhibits the growth of tested bacterial and cancer cells with a series of mechanism. The binding of cells occurs through van-der-Waals interaction that ruptures the negative surface charge on the cell wall. The main mechanism includes the release of reactive oxygen species (ROS) causing severe damage to mitochondrial Deoxyribonucleic Acid (mtDNA) and sugar backbone of Deoxyribonucleic Acid (DNA), lipids and proteins. We strongly recommend that IL-EO NPs can effectively act as an anticancer drug, especially in treating the MCF-7 human breast tumor cell.

ce:data-availability.ce:para: No data was used for the research described in the article.

ce:section.ce:para: Recent survey quantifies approximately 9.6 million deaths from cancer in 2018, cancer is termed the world’s second-leading cause of death. In accordance to a survey by World Health Organization (WHO), one in six mortality worldwide is caused by cancer [1–3]. One of the major issues in disease treatment is the existence of adverse effects caused by the drug without target specificity, which has contributed to a revolution in the field of anti-neoplastic medicines. Also, chemotherapeutic anticancer drugs used nowadays have non-specificity and non-bioavailability which also resembles toxic to unaffected normal cells and organs [4–6]. As per recent research, lanthanides, and lanthanum oxide-based anticancer drugs efficiently work as anticancer agents towards tumor cells besides causing less toxicity to normal cells [7,8]. Lanthanum oxide nanoparticles (NPs), particularly Eu, Sm, and Gd, acted as a potential agent to control the rise of microbes and pathogens as an antimicrobial agent against antibiotics. Due to half-filled unhybridized f-orbital that produced more positive sites, lanthanides series-based oxides have recently emerged as a valuable source in the biomedical industry [9–11]. Erbium oxide (Er2O3) is said to be one of the most intriguing lanthanides because of its unique properties and reactivity including Er3+ high specific heat capacity and decreased toxicity. It has a significant influence on controlling microbial and cancer cells as a powerful photo-degradation catalyst and biosensor [12,13].

Furthermore, Erbium ions have various energy levels that may be successfully stimulated by certain light wavelengths, resulting in strong emissions in the visible and near-infrared regions. Er2O3 NPs are ideally suited for a range of applications due to this property, including biological imaging and diagnostics, where their strong and tunable emission enables accurate detection and visualization [14]. Furthermore, Er2O3 NPs have shown exceptional up conversion capabilities, converting low-energy photons into higher-energy photons. This phenomenon allows for the efficient utilization of near-infrared light for a range of applications, such as photodynamic therapy and solar energy harvesting. In contrast to Er2O3 NPs, CeO2 NPs lack substantial up conversion capabilities, limiting their potential in the biological area [15]. However, Er2O3 NPs are generally constructed using different techniques, including microwave-assisted techniques, sputtering, sol–gel, co-precipitation, template-assisted, and hydrothermal, yet, these techniques have severe drawbacks. Further, the co-precipitation approach uses NPs nucleation and agglomeration, which is not appropriate for reactants with differing precipitation rates. Hydrothermal requires costlier autoclaves, and reaction rates cannot be measured [16]. The development of contaminated targets due to incorrect gas flow is required for the sputtering process, which also requires an expensive target source [17,18].

Notably, Dedkavo et al. [19] employed a thermal decomposition technique to produce nanoparticles of Er2O3 measuring approximately 10 nm. They investigated the potential harm caused by these Er2O3 NPs in aquatic environments and examined their ability to hinder the growth of four bacterial pathogens, namely S. aureus, P. aeruginosa, E. coli, and E. faecalis. Sasikala et al. [20] presented an easy chemical precipitation method for the synthesis of Er-CuO NPs and demonstrated their effective use in inhibiting various bacterial strains, including E. coli, P. mirabilis, P. aeruginosa, K. pneumonia, S. typhimurium, and A. baumanii. Maria Magdalane et al. [21] also used a hydrothermal technique to create Er-doped CeO2 NPs having a typical particle size of around 35 nm. Er-doped CeO2 NPs were utilized to assess RhB dye degradation, antibacterial activity, and cytotoxicity. However, this method failed to achieve precise control over the size, shape, and structure of the synthesized nanoparticles, which is crucial for enhancing their efficacy in antibacterial and antifungal actions.

In this regard, the creation of an innovative and straightforward nanoscale production method for Er2O3 NPs may pave the way for further investigation into the remarkable properties of these NPs and their prospective uses. Surfactants and polymers have traditionally been used to create morphologically controlled self-assembled NPs, with the polymer or external ions interacting to regulate and stabilize the NPs surface [22,23]. The polyol approach is among the most popular and straightforward ways to implement this tactic. It has become a common practice to create high-quality nanoparticles by utilizing multivalent alcohols that are heated to a high temperature, or so-called polyols [24]. The polyols are considered superior owing to the following characteristic such as i) good solubility of the metal-precursors comparable to water; (ii) an elevated boiling point of about 320 °C; (iii) ease synthesis of metals with its instantaneous reducing behaviour; (iv) surface functionalization coordinating properties and stability of colloidal nanoparticles; and (v) the polyols wide range of applicability from lightweight ethylene (PEG). The polyol chelating properties help to regulate important aspects of the particles development, nucleation, and agglomeration [25,26]. The capacity of polyols to decrease metal solutions quickly to create metal NPs at high temperatures is another significant benefit of polyols. It also includes solvating metal precursors in glycol solvent and heating in a reflux condition [27,28]. Upon raising the temperature, electrons are produced with the breakdown of polyols into small molecules. The produced electrons proceed to the metal cations (M3+) and make them attain the metal (M0) state [29]. Besides, the polyol technique has a wide range of scientific and industrial uses, high yield, and low cost making it particularly well-suited for commercialization.

In this type-script, we describe an expeditious, efficient, and non-polluted method for the extensive synthesis of Er2O3 NPs below 40 nm in size on average. This pure crystal is made using the polyol method by dissolving ErCl3·xH2O in ethylene glycol (EG), adding BMIM-PF6 IL, and then calcinating the mixture at a temperature of roughly 600 °C. The basic characterization of material properties, such as crystal structure, and crystallite size of the Er2O3 NPs, are identified by XRD analysis of the primitive Er2O3 NPs and the impact of adding BMIM-PF6 IL to the ErCl3·xH2O-EG mixture. The absorbance and internal structure of the crystals were obtained using the UV-DRS and FT-IR techniques, respectively. SEM and EDX characterizations were utilized to demonstrate the grain size, structure, morphology, and percentage composition of elements present in the Er2O3 NPs. According to the results of HR-TEM, the diameters of Er2O3 nanoparticles are in the range of 30–40 nm, and that of IL- Er2O3 NPs are 6–15 nm in diameter. The main goal of this study is to promote the biocidal behavior of Er2O3 NPs prepared through a simple one-step polyol method under the influence of BMIM-PF6 IL. The effect of pristine Er2O3 without the addition of IL was also examined, and both samples were evaluated for their antimicrobial and anticancer activity against Escherichia coli (G−), Staphylococcus aureus (G+) bacteria and MCF-7 breast cancer cell line. To understand how Er2O3 NPs have biocidal effects, our research group studied the formation of reactive oxygen species (ROS) in response to the NPs. ROS are molecules that contain oxygen and are highly reactive, such as superoxide, hydroxyl radical, and peroxide. The formation of these molecules can be a sign of oxidative stress and inflammation, and they are known to play a role in various physiological and pathological processes. By detecting the presence of ROS, researchers can gain insight into the mechanism of action of the Er2O3 NPs and how they are effective at killing cells or microorganisms. Specifically, we demonstrate that our significant method produces IL-Er2O3 NPs that are very stable in the solution state and easily dissolved in water, playing an essential role in eradicating both bacterial and cancer cells.

Chemicals such as Erbium chloride (ErCl3·xH2O), Ethylene glycol (C2H6O2), Sodium hydroxide (NaOH), and 1-butyl-3-methylimidazolium hexafluorophosphate BMIM-PF6, analytical grade were bought from Sisco Research Laboratory in Mumbai, India, and have been used as purchased without additional refining for Erbium oxide (Er2O3) NPs synthesis. For performing biological applications chemicals including dimethyl sulfoxide (DMSO), ethylene diamine tetra acetic acid (EDTA), 3-[4,5-dimethylthiazol-2-yl] 2,5-diphenyltetrazolium bromide (MTT), and antibiotic solutions such as Amikacin and Penicillin from Sigma Aldrich Pvt. Ltd. was purchased. IMTECH, Chandigarh, India, supplied Staphylococcus aureus (S. aureus - MTCC No: 96), as gram-positive and Escherichia coli (E. coli - MTCC No: 40), as gram-negative bacteria, tumor cell (MCF-7 human breast cancer cell). All trials in the present study were conducted with deionized water.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Synthesis of Er2O3 NPs using BMIM-PF6 IL
ce:section.ce:para: In this investigation, the conventional polyol method was modified by the addition of ionic liquid as a stabilizing agent to stop NPs growth and to develop a fixed morphology. Briefly, polyol synthesis contains a glycol solution in which the metal precursor is dispersed in a certain ratio followed by heating in a reflux condition. The setup contains 2 g of ErCl3·xH2O dispersed in 50 mL of ethylene glycol (EG), poured into 250 mL beaker and indexed (mixture-A) which is fitted upon a magnetic stirrer affixed with the hot plate. Another beaker containing, (mixture-B).

BMIM-PF6 IL (250 mg) with 4 g of sodium hydroxide (NaOH) and 50 mL of ethylene glycol (EG) was added and heated for 1 h at a temperature below the boiling point of ethylene glycol (EG). Then the beaker containing mixture-B is added slowly to the erbium chloride reaction mixture-B was agitated at 180 °C and left to withstand for 5 h before switching off. The mixture was cooled under natural condition with constant stirring at 650 rpm. During the reaction, the precipitate of Erbium hydroxide (Er(OH)3) is obtained as a result of –OH interaction in NaOH with Erbium (Er3+) ions in the reaction mixture. In order, to purify the synthesized NPs the residue was washed repeatedly with deionized water (DIW), ethanol, and acetone, and dried in an electric oven at 70 °C for a period of 12 h. The resultant residue of Er(OH)3 was placed in a muffle furnace for 10 h at 600 °C (6 °C/min) to generate erbium oxide (Er2O3) nanoparticles, which were termed (IL-EO) nanoparticles (Fig. 1 a). Similar condition was followed, indeed, without the addition of BMIM-PF6 IL to synthesize pristine erbium oxide nanoparticles (PEO- NPs). The powdered samples of PEO and IL-EO NPs were analyzed with various characterization techniques.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Characterization techniques for the synthesized PEO & IL-EO NPs
ce:section.ce:para:
The phase purity, size and shape of PEO and IL-EO NPs were analyzed with the aid of XRD spectroscopy with X'pert PRO analytical diffractometer (Cu-Kα radiation & λ = 1.541 Å) was calculated per the following sheerer equation (1).
(1)

K λ

The oscillations of the M−O bond, homo, and heteronuclear atom were examined utilizing a JASCO (FT-IR-4000) spectrometer (4000 cm−1-400 cm−1) in which the samples are made by adding an adequate amount of KBr, compressed with hydraulic pelletizer into pellets. The optical metrics and energy band gap have been evaluated through percentage absorption which was calculated with the UV–vis DRS technique. Further, the JEOL-JSM-6390-SEM instrument with a 15 kV operating voltage was used to capture the grain size and surface morphology. The synthesized NPs particle size diversity and crystallinity were determined using HR-TEM and SAED patterns operating at 200 kV of accelerating voltage.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Antimicrobial evaluation through agar well diffusion method
ce:section.ce:para: The synthesized PEO and IL-EO NPs antibacterial potency over S. aureus and E. coli as gram-positive and gram-negative bacteria were examined via the Muller Hinton agar well diffusion method. Upon utilizing the nutrient broth method, the bacterial culture was freshly prepared with a concentration of 1.2x108 CFU/mL and incubated at 4 °C. Nutrient Agar media was prepared and sterilized in an autoclave by heating it to 121 °C for 20 min. Wick paper discs with a diameter of 9 mm were cut and disinfected as a loading agent; then, 100 μL bacteria inoculums were added to the hot agar medium and mixed for homogeneity of both the bacterial culture with media and transferred into the Petri plates. Eventually, each Petri plate received 15 mL of nutrient-agar media before being allowed to harden, ensuring a stable media layer in every Petri plate. The disc was subsequently coated with synthesized nanoparticles (PEO and IL-EO) at a dosage of 25 μg/ml, dried and reused. Discs comprising PEO and IL-EO NPs at varied concentrations were placed on cultured petri plates using sterilized forceps [30,31]. In this study, Amikacin, one of the widely used antibiotics of up to 10 mg/mL solution, was used as a positive control. The control and test material injected Petri dishes were kept for 24 h undisturbed at humidified atmosphere. The diameter of such an inhibitory zone in millimeters was measured in this experiment (mm). Triplicates were performed to calculate the mean and its standard deviation.

The in-vitro studies for the synthesized Er2O3 NPs were assessed against human breast cancer (MCF-7). The National Center for Cell Science (NCCS) in Pune, India, supplied these cells, which have been incubated in Eagle medium with fetal bovine serum (10 %). The cultured cells were held inside a humidified environment of 37 °C for two weeks inside an incubator with an O2: CO2 (95:5) ratio. In order to reach 1 × 104 cells mL−1 cell density, with 5 % Fetal bovine serum (FBS) the cultured fluid was diluted, onto 96-well media the cells were seeded and left undisturbed overnight. Afterward, the cells were processed for 48 h with varying dosages of synthesized PEO and IL-EO NPs (6.25, 12.5, 25, 50 & 100 μg/mL). Carefully, 15 μL of the 3-[4, 5-Dimethylthiazol-2-yl] 2, 5- diphenyltetrazolium bromide (MTT) salt was dissolute in phosphate buffer solution (PSB) was introduced dropwise into the culture medium after incubation. Typically, the dissolved MTT salt is given to the tumor cell which produces NADPH, where succinate dehydrogenase and tetrazolium ring breakage transform into insoluble formazan crystals (purple-colored). The absorbance was determined at 590 nm with a UV-absorbance spectrophotometer, afterwards, the insoluble formazan crystals dissolved in DMSO (100 μL) [32]. To ensure the accuracy of this colorimetric investigation, each biological test was conducted in triplets. The percent of cells that were blocked was calculated using the formula (2) below.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Mechanism on the formation of PEO and IL-EO NPs by polyol method
ce:section.ce:para: The polyol process deals with the generation of morphologically controlled NPs from different Erbium precursors which when solvated, owing to the high polarity of ethylene glycol [33]. Since polyols have high polarity and boiling points various less polar inorganic salts can be easily dissolved. In addition, it could become used to synthesize metal and metallic oxide NPs simply upon solvating different sorts of metal (Mx) in water [34]. Therefore, the modified polyol method contains a hydroxyl (–OH) as the originator which could produce M−OH, wherein, polyol would be utilized as a stabilizing agent as well as solvent, erbium chloride as the particular erbium resource. The ionic liquid was used to control NPs growth and to generate NPs with definite shapes [35,36]. The mechanism proposed here suggested that ethylene glycol with.

pKa – 14 ± 1 which is a weak acid transforms into aldehyde forms (B and D) with the help of metal ions (Er3+). Thus, generated aldehydes are easily prone to oxidation, which yields carboxylic acids such as glycolic and oxalic acid (C & E). Subsequently, carboxylic acid oxidation in an alkaline environment yields carbon-di-oxide due to the easy availability of H+ ions in an acidic solution. In an alkaline medium, used glycol exists as glycolic acid in its protonated form and glycolate anion in its deprotonated form. Erbium (Er3+) ions are stabilized by this deprotonated state. The Er3+ ions are then combined with sodium hydroxide generating Er(OH)3. The Er(OH)3 was then calcinated at 600 °C to obtain erbium oxide (Er2O3) nanoparticles. Based on our findings, we proposed the following explanation for the observed phenomena.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Er2O3 NPs formation mechanism via polyol method:
ce:section.ce:para: Ionic liquids are used as a templating and capping agent in the synthesis of nanomaterials [37]. In this research, 1-butyl-3-methylimidazolium hexafluorophosphate BMIM-PF6 was granted as templating and gapping agent, and the technique for synthesizing morphologically designed nanoparticles is depicted in Fig. 1b. Due to poor interaction between the two ions, we claimed that dropwise addition of BMIM-PF6-IL to the reaction mixture generates (BMIM+) cations and (PF6 −) anion. Importantly, the addition of erbium chloride (ErCl3) salt induces the development of erbium (Er3+) cations which are encircled with PF6 − anion, thereby preventing metal ion agglomeration and hindering metal–metal bond formation [37–39]. Likewise, the Er3+ ions attach with the –OH group in NaOH to produce erbium hydroxide (Er(OH)3) and are finally calcined at 600 °C to create Er2O3 NPs with a defined shape attributable to the BMIM-PF6 IL.

The X-ray diffraction (XRD) results were used to determine the crystalline nature, size, and structure of the prepared PEO and IL-EO which are presented in Fig. 2 a. The XRD spectrum reveals miller indices and their respective 2θ values for PEO and IL-EO along with stimulated reference pattern. The resulting data are well indexed with JCPDS No. 77-0777, which encountered the cubic (Bixbyite) phase of Er2O3 NPs [40]. Preferentially we observed 7 reflections and different intensities at 2θ angles of 20.74°, 34.24°, 36.52°, 43.78°, 48.95°, 58.09° and 60.98° that correspond to (211), (222), (420), (431), (440), (611) and (721) lattice plane found in Er2O3 NPs (Fig. 2a(ii)) [41]. Furthermore, two distinct peaks arouse at 2θ values 29.46° and 59.53° respectively with hkl values of (111) and (333) representing Er2O3 cubic structure and high crystallinity [42]. The higher crystallinity of IL-EO NPs than PEO NPs is evidenced by enhancement in intensity (Fig. 2a(iii)). This is because adding BMIM-PF6-IL to the IL-EO NPs changes the arrangement of the atoms, raising their crystallinity. Similarly, the strongest reflection at 34.24°, (222) suggests Er2O3 nanocrystals preferred orientation. It is commonly recognized that the crystalline plane of maximum energy and significant crystallography-induced anisotropy of organic and inorganic materials are connected with the preferred orientation of specific crystalline planes [43,44]. Using the Debye–Scherer formula, the NPs typical crystalline size was estimated from their intense peaks, which were 22.42 and 10.37 nm for PEO and IL-EO NPs. The ionic liquid BMIM-PF6 serves as a stabilizing agent for IL-EO NPs, restricting the growth of the crystal and minimizing its size of the crystal. Due to its atomistic structure and templating capabilities, adding ionic liquid strongly enhanced the crystallinity as well as the minimized size distribution of NPs. Further, the PF6 − negative charge creates physical interaction with Pr3+, which limits the process of nucleation, causing IL-EO NPs with particle size and higher crystallization than PEO NPs as synthesized. This hypothesis leads to IL-EO NPs sticking with superior physical characteristics including crystallinity and large surface area, which promote bacterial and cancer-fighting activity.

FT-IR spectroscopy is considered as quite useful tool, to encounter the functional groups and bond connections, and in quantifying the M−O bond. The broad peak at 3407 cm−1 in the FT-IR spectra of PEO (Fig. 2b(i)) was assigned to the hydroxyl groups –OH stretching mode, which arose from the absorption of moisture by the NPs from the environment [45,46]. Similarly, broadband at 1505 cm−1 is generated when ethylene glycol as well as its oxidized derivatives are present like carboxylic acids and aldehydes that cause C–C stretching vibrations. The peak at 1052 cm−1 is indeed a result of vibration caused by C = O stretching, after the elimination of e− from the carboxyl and aldehyde group present in the ethylene glycol [47,48]. Metal oxide seems responsible for a significant peak at 576 cm−1, indicating the Er-O bond in PEO NPs. Likewise, the FT-IR spectrum of synthesized IL-EO NPs (Fig. 2b(ii)) shows peaks at 3430, 1516, 1319, 1074 and 565 cm−1, which resemble peaks alike those of PEO NPs. The hydroxyl group stretching vibration (O–H) is responsible for the broadband of about 3430 cm−1, while the characteristic peak at 1516 cm−1 corresponds to the C–C stretching vibration possibly occurring by the polyol oxidation. BMIM-PF6 ionic liquids. The terminal methyl’s C–H bending modes (sp3) are responsible for the peak at 1319 cm−1 [49,50]. The CO stretching vibrations of the oxidized form of polyol shows the peak at 1074 cm−1, whereas metal oxide bonds dominate the range between 600 and 300 cm−1 [51,52]. The absorption peak at 565 cm−1 in the PPO, FT-IR spectrum reveals the existence of an Er–O bond. The Er–O bond in IL-EO NPs was moved slightly to a lesser wavenumber, conceivably, as a result of interaction between positively charged Er3+ ions and negatively charged PF6 − in BMIM-PF6 IL. Interaction between ILs improves the arrangement of atom, and crystallinity and cause Er–O bonds to possess a smaller wavenumber.

The correlation among band gap as well as particle size leads us to evaluate the energy band gap calculations. The direct bandgap energy was evaluated to examine, whether, the formed h+/e− in the valance and conduction band in a crystal moment, are equal. [53]. PEO NPs (Fig. 3 a) have a direct energy band gap of 4.32 eV, while IL-EO NPs (Fig. 3b) have a direct energy band gap of 4.65 eV. In the indirect band gap energy calculation, at various momentum levels, the valency and conduction bands respective energy maxima occur [54]. PEO NPs have calculated indirect bandgap energy of 2.4 eV, while IL-EO has a calculated bandgap energy of 2.56 eV (Fig. 3(c&d)). Since the utilization of ionic liquid reduces the particle size of IL-EO NPs, the energy band gap rises due to the effect of quantum confinement. Considerably, a potential shift in valance band maximum and conduction band minimum (VBM & CBM) towards positive and negative values is observed whenever there is electron confinement, resulting in a significant increase in band gap energy [55–57]. Sunlight with high frequencies and smaller wavelength is absorbed owing to the fact that an electron must expend more energy to move from the valence to the conduction band. [58–60]. IL-EO NPs with higher energy band gaps absorb scattered light with high energy from the surroundings than PEO NPs, which would be beneficial for improved biological results, based on quantum theory.

The NPs elemental constituents were investigated from the results of EDX spectroscopy. According to the PEO NPs EDX spectrum, the atomic percentage of erbium (Er) is 41.63 %, and oxygen (O) is 58.37 % (Fig. 5 a). Also, from Fig. 5b EDX images the IL-EO NPs were assessed to contain Erbium (Er) 27.18 % and oxygen (O) 72.82 %. The BMIM-PF6 IL addition to Er2O3 NPs reduces the atomic percentage of erbium and increases the amount of oxygen by limiting the development (M−M bond) of erbium ions (Er3+). The highest concentration of oxygen increases the total amount of active sites, once again justifying its excellence in biomedical activities.

The high resolution-transmission electron microscopy (HR-TEM) images depicted the synthesized PEO and IL-EO NPs particle size (Fig. 6 ). The unavailability of a capping agent increases nanoparticle aggregation in PEO NPs, which is estimated to have an average diameter of 47–94 nm in square shape (Fig. 6a–c). Furthermore, homogenously distributed cube-shaped IL-EO NPs without agglomeration is seen in Fig. 6(d–f) at diverse magnifications (100, 50 and 20 nm). IL-EO NPs are about 6–15 nm in diameter (Fig. 6f), which is lower than the mean of PEO NPs. The capping and templating abilities of BMIM-PF6 ILs may be responsible for the reduction in particle size. In short, IL acts as a particle enlargement stopper and structuring agent by lowering particle size, which reduces Er3+ ion nucleation [63,64].

The selected area diffraction (SAED) pattern analysis displayed the d-spacing values for PEO and IL-EO NPs, both of which match perfectly with the JCPDS card no: 77-0777. Subsequently, the SAED pattern contains several ring-like patterns, these rings exemplified the crystallinity of PEO and IL-EO NPs. Typically, the SAED pattern of PEO NPs demonstrated elliptical rings indexed to the (211), (222), (431) and (440) lattice planes (Fig. 7 a). While IL-EO NPs displayed 5 ring-like diffraction patterns indexed well with (211), (222), (431), (440) and (611) lattice planes of Er2O3 NPs (Fig. 7b). The extra lattice fringes appeared particularly on ionic liquid assisted Er2O3 NPs might be due to the high crystallinity and enhanced nucleation of synthesized NPs. Thus, the lattice plane reflection causes the formation of rings for the above-mentioned simple cubic system of the Er2O3 NPs lattice plane, which is similar to the reported XRD findings.

The particle size distribution analysis is a technique for measuring the effects of mean, maximum, and minimal particle size. Moreover, histogram is generated by plotting the NPs distribution proportion versus the diameter of the particle as calculated from TEM findings. In particular, particle size is very important in limiting harmful pathogens and cancer cells from proliferating [65]. This approach accurately classifies particles in the 0.1–1000 nm range, and the distribution report confirms that using BMIM-PF6 IL has resulted in a remarkable reduction in particle size [66]. PEO NPs mean particle size is predicted as 47 nm, with 5 % of PEO NPs ranging between 30 and 40 nm and lesser than 1 % falling between 90 and 100 nm ± 2 nm (Fig. 7c). They feature slower kinetics against ROS production and cell-inhibiting action due to disorganized particle development and uneven size [67]. After that, IL-EO NPs are detected in the 6–15 nm range, having a mean particle size of 11 nm as well as a 9–10 % dispersion rate (Fig. 7d). The minimum particle size estimated for 1 % of the particles is 18 ± 1 nm, which could be attributable to the use of BMIM-PF6 for limiting the growth of particles and to prevent oversized NPs from spreading. The generated NPs can effectively attach to the negatively charged cell membrane and block the cell membrane through ionic interaction.

The synthesized PEO and IL-EO NPs antibacterial activity was demonstrated against S. aureus (G+) and E. coli (G−) bacterial strains (Fig. 8 ). Those two strains of bacteria could be founded in our environment, including the air, water, and soil. S. aureus (G+) cell walls do seem to be 15–29 nm thick and assembled by several peptidoglycans and teichoic acid layers. Obviously, the layers of teichoic acid contain amino acids, sugar moieties, phosphate, and polyglycerol, besides E. coli cell walls are > 1 nm thin and comprises lipopolysaccharide and phospholipids [68,69]. The inhibited zone in millimeters (mm) at 25 μg/mL concentrations of PEO and IL-EO NPs towards two S. aureus and E. coli pathogens, which can be seen in Fig. 8 (a&b). PEO has an (11 mm dia.) zone of inhibition against S. aureus and a (12 mm dia.) zone of inhibition against E. coli (Fig. 8(c&d)). The antibacterial action of the IL-EO NPs was better towards S. aureus (12 mm dia.) and E. coli (14 mm dia.). Furthermore, both NPs produce a higher inhibited zone (IZ) value for E. coli (G−) due to the thin peptidoglycan cell wall that permits more structural deformation than S. aureus. Overall antibacterial activity of IL-EO NPs towards S. aureus (12 mm dia.) and E. coli (14 mm dia.) was higher.

In this regard, the maximal antibacterial efficacy towards bacterial cells, a variety of methods have been proposed. Firstly, Er2O3 NPs are in the Er3+ oxidation state, and these ions are easily drawn by electrostatic contact with the cell walls negative charge causing bacterial death [70,71]. In a sense, peptidoglycan is comprised of sugars like N-acetylmuramic acid (MurNAc) amino acids and N-acetylglucosamine (GlcNAc) which are repetitive units. Also, there won't be any availability of proton to establish an M−OH bond when Er3+ ions approach N-Acetylglucosamine (GlcNAc). Besides, withdrawing H+ from the methyl group encircled with the (O−) negative group was quite hard. As a result, these ions bind to N-acetylmuramic acid (MurNAc) owing to the most acidic proton (–COOH) to constitute an M−OH bond. The acidity rises because of the + I effect in the neighboring methyl group, turning the cell wall inactive physiologically as well as enabling the penetration of metal ions into the cytoplasm, causing cell lysis [72,73]. Hence, it forms a complex with N-terminal protein moiety, that prevents peptidoglycan as well from the reproduction of bacterial cells, which is another mechanism for cell membrane destruction. Interestingly, the XRD findings of produced IL-EO NPs show a reduced crystalline size of 10.37 nm with a higher energy band gap, indicating that the antibacterial efficacy is increased by forming extra positive holes within the valence band. The NPs can then easily bind the protein via disrupting the cell wall and having thiol or sulfa hydryl groups (-SH), in which the thiol group (S2−) functioned as a Lewis base constructing an interconnected complex. Similarly, O2– forms complexes with carboxypeptidase and phosphate polymerases, such as (O-(Er)-SH/ OH–(Er)-S). Er3+ ions in Er2O3 block DNA replication by destabilizing ATP (Adenosine Triphosphate) synthesis. Protein denaturation is also caused by the hydrogen loss (H) in the protein. As well as the O-atom in Er–O has (2e−) that easily bonds with the weaker hydrogen (H) connection 2nd structured proteins through intermolecular hydrogen bonding. Thus, the breaking of a weak H-bond causes the protein structure as well as the biological process to collapse.

The fundamental process behind IL-EO and PEO NPs antibacterial action lies beneath the formation of reactive oxygen species (ROS). The detailed understanding of the biocidal effect of Er2O3 NPs was depicted in Fig. 11. The ROS formation is caused by the absorption of light which is considered to be the fundamental mechanism underpinning the IL-EO and PEO NPs (photon) antibacterial effect. Species like peroxide, superoxide radical, hydroxyl radical and singlet oxygen, (H2O2, *O2 –, *OH, & 1O2) induce oxidative stress within the cell, damaging enzymes, RNA, and DNA, and causing live cells to die. In short, negatively charged species such as singlet oxygen, hydroxyl and superoxide are repelled by the negatively charged cell membrane and cannot enter the cell wall [74,75]. On the other hand, peroxide (H2O2) encountered as positively charged species can enter the cell wall causing oxidative stress, which further leads to microbial destruction via blocking replication in DNA [76]. Er2O3 NPs have a cubic structure with noticeable imperfections which are assumed to be oxygen vacancies. This void creates a huge active surface area that produces excess ROS, resulting in oxidative stress and eventually bacterial demise. SEM and TEM investigations demonstrated that synthesized NPs have an improved morphology that improves toxicity. The synthesized IL-EO NPs have superior toxicological properties and cell wall interaction when compared to PEO NPs. Furthermore, particle nanosize, increased energy band gap, and oxygen vacancy all aided in improvising the antibacterial effectiveness. As a consequence, these NPs could be utilized in biomedical applications as an antibacterial medication.

Rare earth and its oxides have been expressed as a possible cancer therapeutic agent, because of their multiple oxidation states. The Er2O3 NPs produced are less hazardous and more biocompatible, they are well-equipped for biomedical applications [77,78]. The anticancer activity of PEO and IL-EO NPs at different dosages (6.25, 12.5, 25, 50, and 100 μg mL−1) was evaluated using the MTT assay method towards the MCF-7 tumor cell line. The optical microscopic images were depicted in Fig. 9 . PEO NPs suppresses cells by 11.47 %, 18.73 %, 26.95 %, 40.34 %, and 53.72 %, respectively (Fig. 10 (a&b)), possessing the IC50 value of 79.54 μg/mL. IL-EO NPs have cell inhibition activity of 14.72 %, 22.94 %, 32.69 %, 46.08 %, and 59.46 %, respectively, possessing the IC50 value of 59.88 μg/mL. Notably, the drugs possessing IC50 values lower the 100 μg/mL could be used as antitumor agents [79]. The MCF-7 cell inhibition percentage improved as the test material concentration increased, as shown in Fig. 10a, confirming the test material dose-dependent performance. Comparing IC50 values for both test compounds, IL-EO NPs exhibited 44.83 μg/mL lower value than PEO NPs (Fig. 10b), suggesting that they could be used as an anticancer treatment against the MCF-7 cell line. In a 100 μg mL−1 concentration, IL-EO NPs cause 59.46 % cell apoptosis, whereas PEO NPs causes 53.72 % cell death, as seen in the bar diagram (Fig. 10a). The generated ROS are considered membrane-impervious, owing to their “-charge” which improves the breast cancer cell antitumor activity. As a result, ROS inhibits cell growth via damaging protein structures, carbohydrates, and glycolipids in the cell membrane, injuring deoxyribonucleic acid (DNA) present inside the cell and mitochondrial DNA (mt-DNA). The superoxide anion (*O2 –) highly impermeable species and unstable which makes up 1–5 % of all ROS species formed [80,81]. As an outcome, superoxide was delivered into the mitochondrial matrix, in which it undergoes enzymatic conversion to hydrogen peroxide (H2O2) by superoxide dismutase. In accordance with the Fenton process, (OH–) Hydrogen peroxide (H2O2), an oxidative stress producer, disperses inside the cell wall to generate hydroxyl ions and radicals [82]. When free radicals like *OH interact with hydrogen (H) present inside the phosphate sugar base, they oxidize nucleoside bases in DNA, causing damage to the sugar backbone. In addition to that, ROS could disrupt the DNA double helix by breaking peroxiredoxin-2 oligomers and initiating strand breaks with 2-deoxyribose hydrogen hydroxyl radical abstraction [83].

Another mechanism involves ROS stimulated cancer cell lysis causing damage to mtDNA. In addition, mitochondrial, usually called membrane-bound organelles, play a key role in the development of the cell by generating chemical energy needed for cell-biochemical processes such as heat generation [84]. Preferentially, small molecules like ATP have a phosphate (P) atom with high energy bonds and have been used to store chemical energy. Further, the mtDNA dysfunction was enhanced by producing strand breakage, chain disruption, and deterioration. As an outcome, mitochondrial dysfunction inhibits cell reproduction and development by limiting energy production and moving to other cell parts [85,86]. Finally, elevated levels of ROS aided MCF-7 cancer cell lysis by damaging the mtDNA, DNA, nucleus, malfunction, and protein destruction due to disulfide (R1-S-S-Rll) bond breakage, and metabolic mechanism inhibition (Fig. 11 ). Because of the aforementioned qualities, IL-EO can be employed as a biomedical therapy, mainly for treating the MCF-7 breast cancer cell line.

On the whole, using BMIM-PF6 IL-mediated single step polyol method, we effectively achieved Er2O3 NPs for excellent antimicrobial and as a drug in chemotherapy. The polyol approach is a suitable and efficient method for synthesizing NPs, where EG serves as both the reducing agent and solvent, resisting the necessity of harmful inorganic compounds. The use of BMIM-PF6 IL as a growth stopper has proven to be effective, harmless to people, and cost-effective. Er2O3 nanoparticles synthesis mechanism in polyol process and also BMIM-PF6 ionic liquids templating mechanism were investigated in this hypothesis. XRD, UV-DRS, FT-IR, SEM with EDX, and TEM analysis are employed to examine the physical properties of synthesized Er2O3 NPs. PEO NPs has a median crystalline size of 22.42 nm, while IL-EO NPs have a typical crystal size of 10.37 nm which itself is in agreement with TEM findings. The FT-IR spectra confirm the availability of Er-O stretching frequency in Er2O3 NPs. The direct and indirect energy band gap measurements suggest that IL-EO NPs require relatively greater excitation energy levels to complete electronic transition than PEO NPs. The appearance of needled bushy morphology with little aggregation was detected by SEM examination, which was significantly repaired by using BMIM-PF6-IL as an expansion blocker. According to the EDX analysis, IL-EO nanoparticle offers additional sites that bind oxygen than PEO NPs. The average particle size of PEO NPs was 47 nm, while BMIM-PF6 being added as a capping agent, substantially, decreased IL-PO NPs particle size to 12 nm, as per the TEM and distribution analysis studies. The SAED patterns indicate lattice planes (211), (222), (431), (440) and (611), confirming the Er2O3 NPs simple cubic system. They demonstrated high antibacterial performance against the pathogens S. aureus and E. coli. They were tested whether the NPs can suit as a drug for tumor therapy for the treatment of MCF-7 breast cancer cells. Thus, IL-EO NPs showcased a better cell inhibition value and better antitumor effectiveness when used towards the MCF-7 cell line. Consequently, our research group suggests that IL-EO NPs generated with BMIM-PF6 IL are a potential biomedical drug since they have outstanding antibacterial and anticancer capabilities, enabling them as a useful nano-drug in the biomedical field.

Surya Azhagar: Conceptualization, Methodology, Investigation, Writing – original draft, Formal analysis. Dhilip kumar Chinnalagu: Validation, Resources, Writing – review & editing. Arumugam Mayakrishnan: Methodology, Formal analysis. Mahalingam Sundrarajan: Validation, Visualization, Supervision.
